channel.title: maGaming RSS Feed - techraptor.net
channel.description: A cleaned-up version of the original gaming feed for techraptor.net

item.title: Buyer's Guide to The One Ring Vs The Lord of the Rings: Roleplaying
item.content:encoded:
When it comes to Fantasy, there's no bigger name than Tolkien. Despite being nearly a hundred years old at this point, the story has proven its staying power with generation of readers, listens, watchers, and players. Of course, that doesn't mean that it's as simple as picking up The Lord of the Rings: The Roleplaying Game and sitting down to a game with friends. To help you out, we've provided this buyer's guide to The One Ring Vs The Lord of the Rings: Roleplaying. 

We've delved deep into the two system, the different starts kits available, and a couple of the supplements to provide you with the info you need to make sure that you're making an informed decision.

What's the Different Between The One Ring and The Lord of the Rings: Roleplaying?

If you're a newcomer to tabletop roleplaying, or someone who's experience starts and ends with Dungeons & Dragons, you may be forgiven for not realising just how deep the world of licensed TTRPGs goes. Currently, there are two different systems, both based on Tolkien's Legendarium, but more specifically, in that time period between The Hobbit and The Lord of the Rings (Basically the only time it's possible to run a normal level tabletop campaign that doesn't feel like you're watching gods destroy each other or nothing happens at all.)

The first to be released was The One Ring, which is a more complex game whose mechanics are directly included by Tolkien's works. The follow up, released much more recently, is The Lord of the Rings: Roleplaying, which takes the basic rules of 5e Dungeons & Dragons and adds a few borrowed mechanics from The One Ring. Both games deal with journeys, fellowships and councils, but The One Ring has it's own suite of mechanics related to die-rolling and deriving successes, while LOTR: Roleplaying staples those common mechanics onto the original D&D 5E ruleset.

Journeying rules relate to how your characters traverse the wide world, something you'll be doing a hell of a lot of if you're playing Tolkien right. Fellowship is a set of mechanics related to your party and how they relate to each other, and finally Councils are scenes between journeys and fights where the PCs will have to converse with someone in a position of some sort of authority to try and get something they need/want. The biggest difference with The One Ring is that it uses exclusively D6 and D12 die, and the number of die you roll is dependent on whatever stat you're rolling for, plus or minus various modifiers.

As with any relatively similar TTRPG release like this, both games have very similar starter kits. They're both called Over Hill and Under Hill and both largely have the very same components. You get a set of pre-generated characters, and a simple adventure, both of which are designed to walk you through the mechanics of the world and to give the players and GM a chance to practise before jumping into the deep end. You also get a whole suite of maps and a bunch of cardboard standees to help you represent your characters in battle.

The key difference in these two starter kits is mostly related to how restrictive these starter kits are. While they both only feature stripped-down versions of the core rules, the 5E rules do give you enough detail on things like levelling up so that you could feasibly continue to play without buying the core set, but TOR very much doesn't. Part of the reason for that is that because LOTR: RP is built around D&D 5E, it's expecting you to have a working knowledge of that system, so even if you have the core rulebook you'll be missing half of the rules. So, if you're already into D&D, the starter kit gives you most of the tools you need to continue a campaign, but in fairness to TOR, that game's mechanics are much more nuanced and fit with the Tolkien theme much better.

The One Ring Vs The Lord of the Rings: Roleplaying - Shire Adventures and Hobbit Tales

Just like the starter kit, a hobbit-themed supplements has been released for both games. Shire Adventures is the 5E version, while TOR has Hobbit Tales, not to be confused with the supplemental board game of the same name which also can be used with TOR. Once again, both books are basically identical in terms of the text with the only notable difference being the inclusion of D&D stat-blocks in Shire Adventures, and TOR ones in Hobbit Tales.

Both supplements provide a lot of background detail for The Shire, including detailed descriptions of all four farthings and the old wood, as well as some information on various hobbit characters that you can both interact with and play as. Of course, famous characters like Bilbo Baggins show up as well, and each book provides a short series of adventures across The Shire and a little bit beyond it. Beyond that, the biggest physical difference between both editions is the matt, cloth-like finish of Hobbit Tales, while Shire Adventures retains the glossy finish of the rest of The Lord of the Rings: Roleplaying books. 

The One Ring Vs The Lord of the Rings: Roleplaying - Hands of The White Wizards Vs Trials of Saruman

Third verse, same as the first; TOR and LOTR: RP's Saruman-themed supplements are pretty much identical in their content outside of the swapped stat-blocks, though this time the stories, adventures, and supplemental lore all focus on the white wizard: Saruman. TOR provides us with Hands of the White Wizard, while LOTR: RP goes for Trials of Saruman, and while both books are practically identical to each other, they differ in structure to the Hobbit-themed supplements. 

This time around, you get a much more limited set of supplemental information about Saruman and Isengard, with much of the books given over to a set of 6 quests that the PCs can be sent on. All of the 6 quests are multi-stage adventures that take some time to complete, so if you've already got a group, this is a great buy to help fill out an upcoming campaign or series of games. Once again, it should be noted that as well as different cover art, the TOR book has a matt finish while the LOTR: RP has a glossy one. 

Which One Should I Get?

Now comes the all-important question: which version of these kits/supplements should I get? The answer to that comes down to what sort of situation you're starting from.

If you've never played a TTRPG before, then your options are mixed at best. TOR is a great game, but may be hard to follow for first-timers, and LOTR: RP requires you to have an active knowledge of D&D 5E to really make the most out of it. 

If the only TTRPG you've played before is D&D 5E then your best bet will be the LOTR: RP Starter Kit and supplements. These provide an easy-to-understand modification of 5E rules that translate well into the world of Middle-Earth for fans of the books or the films.

Finally, if you're a more experienced RPG player who wants to play a genuinely well-made TTRPG designed from the ground-up to represent Tolkien's Legendarium, then you can't go wrong with TOR. It's got the nuance to handle the themes and stories common to the world, and the mechanics are a darn-site more interesting than 5E, even if they are a smidgen harder to get into at first.

The books in this buyer's guide were provided by Free League Publishing

Will has been writing about video games professionally since 2016 and has covered everything from AAA game reviews to industry events and everything in… More about William
